items.title: Keith-Wagener-Barker classification
items.contentHtml:
Keith-Wagener-Barker Classification

  • Grade I: mild arteriolar narrowing, increased tortuosity. Grade II: Gunn sign and Salus sign, copper thread. Grade III: hard exudates, cottony spots, flame hemorrhages. Grade IV: papilla edema plus grade III findings.
  • Prognostic correlation: grades III-IV are associated with hypertensive emergency and greater risk of target organ damage (brain, kidney, heart).
  • Trap: the classification does not include silver thread as independent judgment; This is considered advanced grade II or III depending on the context.

items.title: Gunn's sign
items.contentHtml:
Gunn's sign or arteriovenous nicking

  • Definition: compression of the retinal vein by a sclerosed arteriole at the arteriovenous junction, with thinning of the venous caliber on both sides of the junction.
  • Physiopathology: chronic hypertension causes arteriolar sclerosis; the arterial adventitia thickens and compresses the underlying vein.
  • Ophthalmoscopic finding: the vein appears to “disappear” or thin abruptly at the junction; can be associated with Salus sign.
  • Clinical importance: indicator of chronic hypertensive retinopathy (grade II of Keith-Wagener-Barker).

items.title: Salus sign
items.contentHtml:
Salus sign or venous deviation

  • Definition: acute angle deviation of the retinal vein at the arteriovenous junction, due to traction of the sclerosed arteriole.
  • Physiopathology: hypertensive arteriolar sclerosis causes shortening and stiffness of the artery, which pulls the vein towards its path.
  • Ophthalmoscopic finding: the vein forms an “elbow” or angulation when crossing below or above the artery; can coexist with Gunn's sign.
  • Relevance: classic of grade II hypertensive retinopathy; Its presence suggests long-standing hypertension.

items.title: Copper wire and silver wire
items.contentHtml:
Copper wire and silver wire

  • Copper wire: retinal arteriole with increased central light reflection and copper color due to moderate arteriolar sclerosis. It is seen in grade II hypertensive retinopathy.
  • Silver thread: arteriole with a whitish, filiform appearance, with total loss of the central reflex; indicates severe arteriolar sclerosis (grade III or IV of Keith-Wagener-Barker).
  • Physiopathology: thickening of the arteriolar wall due to deposition of collagen and hyaline, which opaques the blood column.
  • Trap: silver thread is not synonymous with hypertensive retinopathy malignant; can be seen in advanced chronic hypertension without papilledema.

items.title: Cottony stains
items.contentHtml:
Cottonous spots due to ischemia

  • Definition: whitish retinal lesions, with diffuse edges, which correspond to infarcts of the nerve fiber layer due to occlusion of precapillary arterioles.
  • Physiopathology: local ischemia interrupts axoplasmic flow, accumulating intracellular material (bodies cytoid).
  • Location: in the posterior pole, around the optic disc; they do not respect the fovea.
  • Differential diagnosis: diabetic retinopathy (also presents cotton-wool spots), HIV, lupus, severe anemia.
  • Key examination: in hypertensive retinopathy they appear in grade III or IV; Its resolution leaves nerve fiber layer defects.

items.foco: Hard exudates due to retinal edema
items.title: hard exudates
items.contentHtml:
Hard exudates due to retinal edema

  • Definition: yellowish lipid deposits, with sharp edges, in the outer retina, secondary to chronic edema and lipoprotein leakage.
  • Pathophysiology: hypertension damages the external blood-retinal barrier, allowing extravasation of lipid-rich material that is reabsorbed, leaving deposits.
  • Distribution: frequently in the shape of a “macular star” (in the fovea) or in a ring around areas of edema.
  • Differentiation: unlike cotton-like spots, hard exudates are shiny, yellowish and with defined edges; they persist longer.
  • Treatment: control of blood pressure; They may resolve slowly, but macular star can leave permanent visual impairment.

items.title: retinal hemorrhages
items.contentHtml:
Flame and punctate hemorrhages

  • Flame hemorrhages: shaped like a tongue or splinter, located in the layer of nerve fibers; They follow the orientation of the fibers. They indicate acute retinal ischemia.
  • punctate (or spot) hemorrhages: rounded, deeper (inner or outer layers); they may be indistinguishable from those of diabetic retinopathy.
  • Physiopathology: rupture of retinal capillaries due to increased hydrostatic pressure or fibrinoid necrosis in hypertensive emergency.
  • Key differentiation: in hypertensive retinopathy hemorrhages are more frequent on fire; In diabetic retinopathy punctate retinopathy and microaneurysms predominate.

items.title: Papilla edema
items.contentHtml:
Papilla edema in hypertensive emergency

  • Definition: swelling of the optic disc due to ischemia and intracranial hypertension secondary to hypertensive emergency (blood pressure >180/120 mmHg with organ damage).
  • Ophthalmoscopic finding: blurred optic disc, elevated, with loss of nasal edge; It may be accompanied by peripapillary hemorrhages and hard exudates in a macular star.
  • Clinical correlation: defines grade IV hypertensive retinopathy (malignant); requires immediate reduction of BP (not >25% in the first hours) to avoid cerebral infarction or blindness.
  • Differential diagnosis: papilledema due to intracranial hypertension (without systemic hypertension); ischemic optic neuropathy does not usually have peripapillary flame hemorrhages.

items.title: Damage to target organ
items.contentHtml:
Correlation with target organ damage

  • Hypertensive retinopathy is a marker of systemic microvascular damage. Grade III-IV is associated with left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic kidney disease and cerebrovascular accident.
  • Complementary studies: echocardiogram for LVH, creatinine and proteinuria for kidney damage, microalbuminuria as an early marker.
  • Management: the presence of hypertensive retinopathy (even grade I-II) justifies initiation or intensification of antihypertensive treatment to reduce global cardiovascular risk.
  • Cheating examination: the absence of hypertensive retinopathy does not rule out organ damage white; hypertension can affect the kidney or heart without retinal changes.

items.title: RD vs HR differentiation
items.contentHtml:
Differentiation of diabetic retinopathy

  • Hypertensive retinopathy (HR): Gunn's sign, Salus sign, copper/silver thread, flame hemorrhages, hard exudates in macular star, papilla edema in cases severe.
  • Diabetic retinopathy (DR): microaneurysms (earlier finding), punctate hemorrhages, hard ring exudates, neovascularization (proliferative DR), macular edema.
  • Differential key: microaneurysms are rare in pure RH; The presence of arteriovenous nicking is typical of RH. Neovascularization guides DR.
  • Frequent comorbidity: HTN and DM coexist; The findings overlap. Evaluate Gunn signs and microaneurysms to discriminate.

items.title: Eye ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Ocular ultrasound for exudative detachment

  • Indication: when the fundus is not visible due to media opacity (cataract, vitreous hemorrhage) or to confirm exudative retinal detachment in hypertensive emergency.
  • Ultrasound finding: detachment exudativeseen as raised retinal membrane with anechoic subretinal fluid, without associated tear. There may be choroidal thickening.
  • Differential diagnosis: rhegmatogenous detachment (with tear) and tractional detachment (in proliferative DR).
  • Management: exudative detachment due to hypertensive retinopathy resolves with strict blood pressure control; does not require surgery.

items.foco: Treatment: strict control of blood pressure
items.title: Antihypertensive treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: strict control of blood pressure

  • Goal: reduce BP to <130/80 mmHg in the majority of patients with hypertensive retinopathy (ACC/AHA recommendation).
  • Hypertensive emergency (grade IV): initial reduction of BP no >25% in the first 2 hours with labetalol IV or nitroprusside; avoid cerebral hypoperfusion.
  • First line in chronic hypertension: thiazides, ACEI/ARB, calcium channel blockers or combination; individualize according to comorbidity.
  • Trap: the use of beta-blockers is not first line in isolated hypertensive retinopathy, unless there is ischemic heart disease or tachyarrhythmia.
